race.name: Dreilander Giro
race.tagline: Three countries. 48 hairpins up the Stelvio. 3,000 riders from 30 nations. The Alpine tri-border gran fondo that sells out in hours.

vitals.field_size: 3,000 riders (1,500 per route, sells out within hours)
vitals.registration: Online via dreilaendergiro.at. Opens October 1 each year. Three ticket tiers of 500 spots per route. Sells out within hours or days. Non-profit pricing from ~EUR94. 15% discount for public transport or carshare travel.
vitals.feed_zones: 6 feed stations on Route A (Stelvio Engadin), 4 on Route B (Stelvio Vinschgau). Well-stocked with food, water, and mechanical support.
vitals.cutoff_time: Time limits at checkpoints along the route. Stelvio Pass must be reached before road closure.
route_options: Route A — Stelvio Engadin: 168 km (104 mi), 3,600 m gain — the full route through all three countries via Stelvio, Umbrail, and Ofenpass
Route B — Stelvio Vinschgau: 120 km (75 mi), 3,000 m gain — Stelvio and Umbrail, returning via Vinschgau

climate.description: Late June in the high Alps delivers a wide range of conditions within a single ride. The 6:30 AM start in Nauders (1,394m) is cool (8-14C). Valley sections in the Italian Vinschgau warm quickly (20-28C). The Stelvio at 2,757m can be near freezing with fierce wind chill, even in summer. Snow on the Stelvio's upper hairpins is not uncommon in late June. The route crosses the main Alpine divide, exposing riders to different weather systems on the Austrian and Italian sides.
challenges: Early morning start at 6:30 AM in an Alpine valley at 1,394m — cool to cold conditions
Stelvio summit at 2,757m: wind chill can bring effective temperature near freezing even in June
48 exposed hairpins on the Stelvio — no shelter from sun, wind, or rain on the upper switchbacks
Rapid weather changes crossing the Alpine divide between Austria, Italy, and Switzerland
Afternoon thunderstorms common above 2,000m — the Ofenpass and Norbertshöhe are exposed

terrain.surface: Well-maintained Austrian, Italian, and Swiss mountain roads. All tarmac throughout. The Stelvio Pass road is the centerpiece — the classic 48-hairpin ascent from Prad is one of cycling's most iconic stretches of asphalt. Road quality varies from excellent (Swiss Engadin roads) to good (Italian mountain roads with occasional patches).
features: Reschenpass (1,504m) — opening climb from Nauders, the Austria-Italy border crossing at Lago di Resia
Passo dello Stelvio (2,757m) — 24.9 km at 7.4% from Prad, 48 numbered hairpins, one of Europe's highest paved passes
Umbrail Pass (2,501m) — descent from near the Stelvio summit into Switzerland's Val Mustair
Ofenpass / Pass dal Fuorn (2,149m) — 13.3 km at 5.8% through the Swiss National Park (Route A only)
Norbertshöhe (1,405m) — 5.8 km at 6.4%, the final climb before descending to Nauders

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 79. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 76.

course_description.character: A geographic masterpiece disguised as a bike ride. Route A (Stelvio Engadin) is the complete experience: 168 km through Austria, Italy, and Switzerland, with the Stelvio as the centerpiece and the Engadin Valley as the reward. Route B (Stelvio Vinschgau) delivers the Stelvio and the Umbrail in a more compact 120 km format. Both routes start and finish in Nauders at 1,394m. The signature challenge is obvious — the Stelvio's 48 hairpins from Prad, 24.9 km at 7.4% average, ascending to 2,757m. But the route's genius is everything around the Stelvio: the approach through the Italian Vinschgau, the Umbrail descent into Switzerland, and (on Route A) the long Engadin traverse before the final push over the Norbertshöhe.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Stelvio from Prad. 48 hairpins. 24.9 km at 7.4% average. 2,757 meters above sea level. This is the climb that has decided Giro d'Italia stages, broken professional cyclists, and inspired a million cycling photographs. On the Dreiländergiro, you ride it in a mass-start event with 1,500 others, counting down the numbered hairpins from 48 to 1. The upper switchbacks above treeline — exposed to weather, short of oxygen, spectacular in every direction — are the most dramatic kilometers in European sportive cycling.
suffering_zones: Stelvio to Umbrail Pass and Val Mustair

logistics.airport: Innsbruck (INN) — 1.5 hours (closest, limited international flights). Munich (MUC) — 3.5 hours (best connections). Zurich (ZRH) — 3 hours. Train to Landeck-Zams station, then free Postbus with bike transport to Nauders.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Nauders — the start/finish village with 209 accommodations and 4,200 beds. Book immediately upon securing your entry. Alpine guesthouses (Pensionen) offer the best value at EUR60-90/night. Hotels run EUR100-200/night. The GiroDays festival week starts Tuesday before the Saturday race — arrive early for the training rides and atmosphere. Nearby alternatives: Reschen/Resia (10 min, Italian side) or Pfunds (20 min down-valley).
logistics.food: Tyrolean Alpine cuisine — Kaspressknödel (cheese dumplings), Tiroler Gröstl (pan-fried potato and meat), Kaiserschmarrn (shredded pancake), strudel. Italian influence from across the Reschenpass — pasta, pizza, South Tyrolean Speck. The GiroDays program includes food-focused events. Feed stations on course provide a mix of Austrian and Italian provisions.

history.origin_story: The Dreiländergiro was born in 1994 when the Bikeclub Nauders — a small, non-profit cycling club in a Tyrolean village of 1,500 people — created a road cycling marathon that would exploit Nauders' unique geographic position at the tri-border point of Austria, Italy, and Switzerland. The concept was simple and irresistible: start in Austria, cross into Italy, climb the legendary Stelvio Pass, descend into Switzerland, and return to Nauders — three countries, one ride. The Stelvio, already hallowed by decades of Giro d'Italia history, gave the event instant credibility. What began as a local cycling adventure grew steadily through the 2000s and 2010s, propelled by word of mouth in the German-speaking cycling world. By the 2020s, the event had become one of Europe's largest cycling marathons — 3,000 riders from 30 nations, selling out within hours. The 30th edition in 2023 was a milestone celebration. The 33rd edition in 2026 continues the tradition.
notable_moments: 1994: Inaugural Dreiländergiro — Bikeclub Nauders creates the tri-border Alpine marathon concept
Growth from local event to one of Europe's largest cycling marathons — 3,000 riders from 30 nations
Introduction of two route options: Stelvio Engadin (168 km) and Stelvio Vinschgau (120 km)
2023: 30th anniversary edition — milestone celebration of three decades of tri-border cycling
Green event certification — pioneering sustainable travel incentives including free Postbus and carshare discounts
Registration begins selling out within hours, with 1,500 bibs per route across three ticket tiers
history.reputation: The definitive tri-border Alpine cycling experience. In DACH cycling culture, the Dreiländergiro is the event that combines the Stelvio's legendary status with the unique adventure of riding through three countries in a single day. Organized by a non-profit cycling club that treats the event as a passion project, not a business.

biased_opinion.summary: The Dreiländergiro is what happens when a small Austrian cycling club in a village of 1,500 people decides to build one of Europe's greatest road cycling events — and succeeds for 30 years running. Three countries. The Stelvio. 3,000 riders from 30 nations. Sells out in hours. The route from Nauders crosses into Italy at the Reschenpass, climbs the legendary 48 hairpins of the Stelvio to 2,757m, drops into Switzerland over the Umbrail Pass, and returns through the Engadin and over the Norbertshöhe. It is not the longest Alpine marathon (the Otztaler is longer), not the most famous (La Marmotte and L'Etape are bigger names globally), but the Dreiländergiro may be the most complete Alpine cycling experience available in a single day: three countries, three languages, five major passes, and the single most iconic road in professional cycling.
strengths: The Stelvio: 48 hairpins to 2,757m — cycling's most legendary climb, included on both route options
Three countries in one ride — Austria, Italy, and Switzerland with distinct landscapes and culture at each border
30+ years of organizational experience from a passionate non-profit cycling club
Two route options (168 km and 120 km) give riders realistic choices without compromising the Stelvio centerpiece
GiroDays festival week with training rides, workshops, and community events
weaknesses: Sells out within hours of registration opening — must be ready on October 1 or miss out entirely
Remote Tyrolean location — no major airport nearby, significant ground transfer required
Stelvio congestion — 1,500 riders on narrow switchbacks creates bottleneck moments on the climb and descent
Limited global recognition outside the DACH cycling world — deserves more attention than it receives
Late June Stelvio weather is volatile — snow on the upper hairpins is a real possibility
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you have ever looked at a photograph of the Stelvio's 48 hairpins and thought 'I need to ride that,' the Dreiländergiro is the best way to do it: fully supported, through three countries, with 3,000 fellow believers. Set an alarm for October 1.

final_verdict.one_liner: Three countries. The Stelvio at 2,757m. 48 hairpins. Since 1994. Europe's tri-border Alpine classic.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if the Stelvio is on your bucket list and you want to ride it in a fully supported, mass-start event through three countries. Yes if you thrive on major Alpine climbing and want 3,600m of vertical gain in a single day. Skip if you cannot commit to the October 1 registration scramble. Skip if remote Austrian Alpine logistics are a dealbreaker.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want the Stelvio in a different format: Granfondo Stelvio Santini (shorter, Stelvio-focused). If you want similar Alpine marathon challenge: Otztaler Radmarathon (227 km, 5,500m, harder). If you want the iconic Italian gran fondo experience: Maratona dles Dolomites (Dolomite scenery, lottery entry). If you want a shorter Alpine classic: Engadin Radmarathon (nearby, less climbing).
